title: Breaking Through in Normandy
story: The skies roared with the sound of planes. On July 25, 1944, American forces launched Operation Cobra, a massive push to break out of Normandy. Waves of bombers pounded enemy lines, shaking the earth. This air attack marked the start of a major offensive, helping Allied troops finally punch through German defenses after weeks of tough fighting.
activity_question: What was the name of the operation that began on July 25, 1944?
follow_up_question: Why do you think the Allies chose to start with an air attack?

title: FDR's Bold Move Against Japan
story: Tensions were rising between the United States and Japan. On July 25, 1941, President Franklin D. Roosevelt took a strong stand. He banned the sale of benzine and gasoline to Japan. This decision was a clear message that America wouldn't support Japan's actions in Asia. It was a crucial moment that would shape the coming months of world history.
activity_question: What did President Roosevelt ban selling to Japan?
follow_up_question: Why do you think banning fuel sales to Japan was such an important decision?

title: Jazz Hits the Airwaves in Holland
story: Swing music filled the air as Duke Ellington's jazz orchestra made history. On July 25, 1933, the legendary American bandleader brought his smooth sounds to Dutch radios for the very first time. Listeners across the Netherlands tuned in, tapping their toes to the live broadcast. This groundbreaking concert introduced many Dutch people to the exciting world of jazz music.
activity_question: Who led the jazz orchestra in this historic Dutch radio concert?
bonus_fact: Duke Ellington was known for his unique piano playing style and composing skills.

title: Napoleon's Victory at Abukir
story: Swords clashed and cannons roared as Napoleon Bonaparte led his troops to victory! On July 25, 1799, French and Egyptian forces under Napoleon's command defeated the Turkish army at the Battle of Abukir. This important win helped secure France's position in Egypt during Napoleon's ambitious campaign in the region.
activity_question: Who led the French and Egyptian forces to victory at the Battle of Abukir?
follow_up_question: Why do you think Napoleon wanted to control Egypt?

title: Legendary Clipper's Final Voyage
story: Waves crashed as the Marco Polo, once the world's fastest clipper ship, met its fate. In 1883, the mighty vessel ran aground on Prince Edward Island's shores. Spectators gathered to witness this historic moment on July 25, including a young Lucy Maud Montgomery, who would later become a famous author.
activity_question: Who was among the spectators of the Marco Polo's grounding?
bonus_fact: Lucy Maud Montgomery later wrote the beloved book 'Anne of Green Gables'.

title: A Royal Chapel Rises in Cambridge
story: King Henry VI had a grand vision. In 1446, he laid the foundation stone for King's College Chapel in Cambridge. This marked the start of a magnificent building project. The chapel would become one of England's finest medieval structures. Though begun on July 25, it took nearly 70 years to complete the main part.
activity_question: What did King Henry VI do in 1446?
activity_choices: Laid the foundation stone for King's College Chapel
Crowned the new king of England
activity_answer: Laid the foundation stone for King's College Chapel
bonus_fact: The chapel's fan-vaulted ceiling is the largest of its kind in the world.
